explanation:
The input requirement is that the system shall raise an alarm whenever a parameter value goes outside the range defined for its associated alarm. In this context, an alarm is indicated by the atomic proposition 'paramAlarm_V'. A parameter being out of range is indicated either by 'param_V_lessThan_paramMin_V' (value below minimum) or 'param_V_greaterThan_paramMax_V' (value above maximum).

Therefore, the property needed is: whenever the parameter value is either less than the minimum or greater than the maximum, then eventually an alarm for erroneous parameter value must be active. This is captured in LTL as:

G( (param_V_lessThan_paramMin_V | param_V_greaterThan_paramMax_V) -> F paramAlarm_V )

This means: Globally, whenever the parameter is out of range, eventually (at some future point) the corresponding alarm must be raised. This accurately captures the requirement that for any out-of-range condition, the system responds by raising the alarm.

Alternative LTL properties might forget to use eventuality (F), or might incorrectly use implication direction, or not cover both out-of-range conditions. This property covers both and uses 'eventually' to allow for possible implementation delays but ensures compliance.

The specification requires that 'the system shall raise an alarm when a parameter value goes outside the range defined for its associated alarm.' From the atomic propositions, violations of the allowed parameter range are signified by either 'param_V_lessThan_paramMin_V' (parameter is below minimum) or 'param_V_greaterThan_paramMax_V' (parameter above maximum). The 'paramAlarm_V' proposition indicates the alarm for an erroneous parameter value is active. Thus, the LTL formula states: 'Whenever the parameter value goes below minimum OR above maximum, eventually the alarm for an erroneous parameter value becomes active.' This captures the intent that any out-of-range parameter should eventually cause the alarm to be raised. No previously given formulas, so only a direct mapping is needed.

The natural language requirement specifies that whenever a parameter value goes outside its alarm-defined range (either below its minimum or above its maximum), the system must raise an alarm. The available atomic propositions param_V_lessThan_paramMin_V (parameter is below min) and param_V_greaterThan_paramMax_V (parameter is above max) capture the two possible out-of-range conditions. The proposition paramAlarm_V indicates that an alarm for an out-of-range parameter is active (i.e., the system is raising an alarm for this parameter condition). Therefore, the correct LTL property should state that globally, if a parameter value is less than its minimum or greater than its maximum, then (immediately or in the next step) the parameter alarm is raised. Here, the implication is that the system shall always (G) ensure that whenever (->) either of the out-of-range conditions ((param_V_lessThan_paramMin_V | param_V_greaterThan_paramMax_V)) holds, the alarm (paramAlarm_V) must be raised at the same time or right after (to account for the possibility of a momentary detection and handling in the next tick). This is best captured by: G((param_V_lessThan_paramMin_V | param_V_greaterThan_paramMax_V) -> F[0:1] paramAlarm_V). This ensures the alarm is raised promptly for any out-of-range parameter event and restricts alarm raising only to those circumstances.

The natural language requirement states that whenever a parameter value goes outside the acceptable range set by its corresponding alarm, the system should raise an alarm. In atomic propositions, there are two that explicitly indicate this out-of-range condition: 'param_V_lessThan_paramMin_V' for when a parameter is too low, and 'param_V_greaterThan_paramMax_V' for when a parameter is too high. The raising of an alarm is most directly represented by 'paramAlarm_V'. The LTL property must ensure that if either 'param_V_lessThan_paramMin_V' or 'param_V_greaterThan_paramMax_V' becomes true, then eventually 'paramAlarm_V' must occur. Expressed in LTL, this is: G((param_V_lessThan_paramMin_V | param_V_greaterThan_paramMax_V) -> F paramAlarm_V). This property ensures globally, whenever the parameter leaves its legal range, the alarm for erroneous parameter value will eventually be activated. This captures the intent of the requirement, because it doesn't permit out-of-range parameter events to occur without being followed by the alarm activation. Alternative LTLs that might, for example, require the alarm to be raised only at the exact instant of the out-of-range event (X paramAlarm_V) would not permit delays or system latencies, so the presented version is the most general and robust for the statement.

The requirement states that whenever any parameter value goes outside the defined range for its associated alarm, the system must raise an alarm. Looking at the provided atomic propositions, 'param_V_lessThan_paramMin_V' and 'param_V_greaterThan_paramMax_V' represent a parameter falling below its minimum or exceeding its maximum threshold for an alarm. 'paramAlarm_V' indicates that an alarm for an erroneous parameter value is active (i.e., the alarm is raised). Thus, the property is best captured by stating: Whenever the parameter is less than the minimum allowed OR the parameter is greater than the maximum allowed, the system shall eventually (as soon as possible, but at least at some point after the condition holds) activate the alarm. This can be expressed in LTL as: G((param_V_lessThan_paramMin_V | param_V_greaterThan_paramMax_V) -> F paramAlarm_V).

This property ensures that for every moment in time, if either boundary violation happens, it is eventually followed by activation of the associated alarm, which matches the intent of the natural language input.

Previously, some formulations might have omitted one of the two conditions (less than minimum or greater than maximum) or failed to link every out-of-bounds event to a subsequent alarm activation. This formulation addresses both directions and maintains the causality required by the requirement.

The natural language requires the system to 'raise an alarm' whenever 'a parameter value goes outside the range defined for its associated alarm.' From the given atomic propositions, going outside the range is represented by either 'param_V_lessThan_paramMin_V' (parameter below minimum) or 'param_V_greaterThan_paramMax_V' (parameter above maximum), and 'paramAlarm_V' represents the alarm being active for an erroneous parameter value. Therefore, the correct LTL specification should require that whenever the parameter goes either below minimum or above maximum, then (in the next state or at the same state) the 'paramAlarm_V' (alarm for parameter value) becomes active. This can be captured with the following formula: 
G(((param_V_lessThan_paramMin_V | param_V_greaterThan_paramMax_V) -> F[0:1] paramAlarm_V))
This means 'globally, whenever a parameter value exceeds its maximum or is less than its minimum, the alarm is active now or in the next instant.'

Alternative properties might have omitted the inclusive 'or' or not tied the alarm activation as tightly to the event (e.g., missing the F[0:1] timing or not covering both less-than-min and greater-than-max cases). This LTL property best matches the stated requirement.

The requirement 'The system shall raise an alarm when a parameter value goes outside the range defined for its associated alarm.' can be interpreted as: Whenever a parameter value is detected to be outside its allowed range (less than minimum or greater than maximum), the system must raise an alarm for that parameter. According to the atomic propositions, 'param_V_lessThan_paramMin_V' signals parameter below minimum, 'param_V_greaterThan_paramMax_V' for above maximum, and 'paramAlarm_V' for alarm being active for that erroneous value. Thus, for all time, whenever ('param_V_lessThan_paramMin_V' or 'param_V_greaterThan_paramMax_V') holds, 'paramAlarm_V' must hold as well. The formula globally enforces that an alarm is raised in every situation in which a parameter value is out of bounds. Previous LTLs may miss this if they did not combine BOTH possible out-of-range conditions or did not enforce alarm for both, or if they allowed a delay or possibility of not engaging the alarm when out of limits.

The natural language requirement states that the system should raise an alarm whenever a monitored parameter value goes outside its prescribed range for alarming. In the provided atomic propositions, the alarm for a parameter value being too low is captured by 'param_V_lessThan_paramMin_V', and for too high by 'param_V_greaterThan_paramMax_V'. The condition that 'an alarm must be raised' when this occurs is captured by 'paramAlarm_V'. Therefore, globally (at all times), whenever ('param_V_lessThan_paramMin_V' or 'param_V_greaterThan_paramMax_V') is true, it must immediately be the case that 'paramAlarm_V' is true (i.e., the alarm is active in that state or the next). This is described in LTL by the formula: Globally, if (out-of-range condition), then alarm is raised. The property is: G((param_V_lessThan_paramMin_V | param_V_greaterThan_paramMax_V) -> paramAlarm_V). This expresses exactly that whenever a parameter is out of range, the corresponding alarm must be raised, directly capturing the requirement without extraneous or omitted behaviors.

The input natural language requires that the system shall raise an alarm if a parameter value goes outside the defined range for its associated alarm. In the available atomic propositions, 'param_V_lessThan_paramMin_V' indicates a parameter value below its minimum threshold, and 'param_V_greaterThan_paramMax_V' indicates a parameter value above its maximum threshold. The proposition 'paramAlarm_V' indicates that an alarm for erroneous parameter value is active. To capture the required behavior, we need to assert that whenever the parameter value is out of range (either less than minimum or greater than maximum), it's always the case that this leads to the alarm being (or having been) raised. This is encoded as: globally, whenever the out-of-range condition occurs, then eventually the parameter alarm is activated. Thus, the property is:

G((param_V_lessThan_paramMin_V | param_V_greaterThan_paramMax_V) -> F paramAlarm_V)

This LTL formula means: for every point in time, if the parameter is out of range, then at some future point, the associated alarm will be (or already is) active. This directly corresponds to the meaning of the requirement.

The natural language states that the system shall raise an alarm whenever a parameter value goes outside its defined range (either below minimum or above maximum values for its associated alarm). The available atomic propositions that map to this concept are 'param_V_lessThan_paramMin_V' (parameter below minimum), 'param_V_greaterThan_paramMax_V' (parameter above maximum), and 'paramAlarm_V' (an alarm for erroneous parameter value is active). To fully capture the requirement, we should state that whenever 'param_V_lessThan_paramMin_V' or 'param_V_greaterThan_paramMax_V' become true, eventually 'paramAlarm_V' should also become true, meaning the alarm is triggered under those conditions. The LTL formula: G((param_V_lessThan_paramMin_V | param_V_greaterThan_paramMax_V) -> F paramAlarm_V) captures this: globally, if a parameter error (low or high) occurs, an alarm will eventually be raised. This aligns with the intent that system alarms follow all out-of-range events.
